entries.title: IHSA boys basketball state finals schedule and scores
entries.summary: <p>The complete schedule and all the scores from the 2026 IHSA state finals at State Farm Center in Champaign.</p><h3><b>Thursday, March 12</b></h3><p><b>Class 1A semifinals</b></p><p>Lawrenceville vs. Goreville, 10 a.m.</p><p>Marshall vs. Eastland, 11:45 a.m.</p><p><b>Class 2A semifinals</b></p><p>Unity vs. Manual, 2:30 p.m.</p><p>Quincy Notre Dame vs. Farragut, 4:15 p.m.</p><p><b>Third place games</b></p><p>Class 1A, 7 p.m.</p><p>Class 2A, 8:45 p.m.</p><h3>Friday, March 13</h3><p><b>Class 3A semifinals</b></p><p>Kaneland vs. Deerfield, 10 a.m.</p><p>East St. Louis vs. Leo, 11:45 a.m.</p><p><b>Class 4A semifinals</b></p><p>Marist vs. York/St. Ignatius winner, 2:30 p.m.</p><p>Benet vs. DePaul Prep, 4:15 p.m.</p><p><b>Third place games</b></p><p>Class 3A, 7 p.m.</p><p>Class 4A, 8:45 p.m.</p><h3>Saturday, March 14</h3><p><b>Title games</b></p><p>Class 1A, 10 a.m.</p><p>Class 2A, noon</p><p>Class 3A, 4 p.m.</p><p>Class 4A, 6 p.m.<br></p>
entries.author: Michael O'Brien

entries.title: Bill Kurtis retires from 'Wait Wait...' NPR quiz show after 12-year run
entries.summary: <p>Longtime Chicago newsman Bill Kurtis, along with his signature baritone pipes, is retiring from his role as judge and scorekeeper on NPR's weekly hour-long news quiz program “Wait Wait... Don’t Tell Me!”</p><p>"It's certainly not for a lack of love," Kurtis said, noting that he gets more positive interaction from being on the show than anything else he's done in his career.</p><p>"But I'm 85, and I'm a healthy 85, thank God, but it is about time I started living like it," Kurtis said, noting that, after his last official show airs the weekend of May 23, he plans to continue contributing to the show on a more limited basis.</p><p>The news was shared in a memo to staff Monday.</p><p>"It's so good to be able to wrap up a 60-year career with a fun show where the reaction is laughing, I mean, that's the job of this thing," said Kurtis, who was a mainstay of Chicago’s television news landscape for decades, appearing at the anchor desk alongside Walter Jacobson on WBBM and nationally with Diane Sawyer on CBS Morning News.</p><p>"I couldn't be happier," Kurtis said, adding that NPR was planning a send off for him. </p><p>"Carl, you know, had dancing girls come out as his colleagues at NPR feted him," he said of his predecessor on the show, the late newcaster Carl Kasell. "I don't know what mine will be. I said, 'Look anything you do is OK with me.'"</p><p>Show host Peter Sagal said he still can't believe sometimes he's had the chance to work with Kurtis for 12 years.</p><p>"I just keep coming back to the fact that: It's Bill Kurtis! I remember meeting Bill Kurtis, God, 25 years ago, maybe, at an event, and going 'Oh my God I'm in a room with Bill Kurtis,'" Sagal said. "And hearing that voice coming out of that face and that hair and I still sometimes have that reaction when I look to my right on stage, and it still sort of surprises and shocks me that I get to do this with a giant of broadcasting."</p> <p>Sagal said it's been a pleasure seeing Kurtis, whose natural sense of humor didn't always come through in news broadcasts, shine on the show.</p><p>"That was a great joy to be able to show that side, or more of that side, to the world for so long," he said.</p><p>The show has rehearsals and tapes on Thursdays before a live audience at the Studebaker Theater in the Loop and airs on weekend mornings.</p><p>The show's members have become like family to Kurtis and his wife, Donna LaPietra.</p><p>Sagal got married in the couple's garden on their 65-acre property in the Northwest Suburbs. And when Kurtis and his wife couldn't make the recent nuptials of another staffer, they hosted the couple and a few dozen others at their home and played the, "The Newlywed Game," with Kurtis playing host.</p><p>"The funny thing about Bill is he's game for anything," said Mike Danforth, the show's executive producer. "Whenever we write something dumb for him to do, he'll do it and he loves it more than any of us."</p><p>Danforth said the "stars aligned" when Kurtis joined the show 12 years ago. </p><p>"The guy's got an incredible voice; so many people have cool voices but not everyone knows how to use them, but he has a great sense of humor, too," he said. </p><p>His voice has been described on the show as the "cause for Aunt Sally to have a sexual awakening."</p><p>Danforth also said Kurtis' improvisational chops will be missed, like the tagline he inserted after reading a standard Subaru promotion on the show: "... and its one hot, bitchin, ride."</p><p>Producers aren't giving any hint as to who might replace Kurtis on the show but one person who has been filling in lately is Alzo Slade, a comedian and Vice news correspondent.</p><p>"Alzo has a great voice and he's a funny guy and I don't know, he's kind of cool, he's cooler than we are," Danforth said.</p>
entries.author: Mitch Dudek

entries.title: Chicago law firm leading push to appoint special prosecutor to target ICE, Border Patrol agents
entries.summary: <p>A high-powered Chicago law firm that fought Operation Midway Blitz in federal court announced a plan Monday to push for the appointment of a special prosecutor to investigate and potentially charge the law enforcement officials who carried out the aggressive deportation campaign in the Chicago area.</p><p>The firm, Loevy and Loevy, said 200 individuals and organizations are supporting a petition to install an outsider in the office of Cook County State’s Attorney Eileen O’Neill Burke, who has spent weeks saying her staff can only go so far to target the feds.</p><p>The coalition plans to hold a news conference Thursday afternoon at Federal Plaza downtown, Loevy and Loevy said in a statement.</p><p>O’Neill Burke rebuked the call for a special prosecutor, slamming any petition as “frivolous [and] contrary to centuries of legal precedent and court rulings.”</p><p>“The reported plan to petition for a special prosecutor will make it more difficult for the Cook County State’s Attorney’s Office to prosecute and win convictions against [Immigration and Customs Enforcement] agents who break the law and secure justice for victims harmed by their conduct,” O’Neill Burke wrote in a statement.</p><p>“The stakes are too high for us to get this wrong, and I will strenuously oppose this petition.”</p><p>In its statement, Loevy and Loevy highlighted the extreme force used by federal immigration officials during the monthslong deportation campaign, referring to the on-duty actions as “crimes.” They cited widespread tear-gassing, attacks against “peaceful civilians” and shootings that wounded <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/02/11/evidence-shows-feds-lied-about-marimar-martinezs-shooting-in-chicago-attorney-says" target="_blank" >Marimar Martinez</a> and killed <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/11/17/silverio-villegas-gonzalez-ice-dhs-trump-midway-blitz-shooting-homicide-franklin-park-chicago" target="_blank" >Silverio Villegas González</a>.</p><p>“Yet there has been no federal or state investigation or prosecution of any [of] the individuals who perpetrated these offenses,” the firm said. “An independent, special prosecutor is essential to ensure that there is accountability in Cook County.”</p><p>The demands for O'Neill Burke’s office to target federal agents are nothing new. In fact, another lawyer already filed a lawsuit earlier this year seeking to install a special prosecutor to handle such cases in county court. </p><p>O'Neill Burke has maintained that her office is limited in its ability to prosecute and secure convictions against on-duty federal agents. Still, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/02/19/chicago-ice-crime-border-patrol-trump-midway-blitz-alex-pretti-renee-good-eileen-oneill-burke" target="_blank" >she released guidance</a> last month showing how she could pursue charges if a case were brought to her office. </p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-bb0000" name="image-bb0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ea5f3b1/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7312x4104+0+387/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F03%2F13%2Fa487f2ae407db04482883ab2e9ef%2Fstatesatty-120324-16.jpg alt="STATESATTY-120324-16.jpg" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7c6a1f7/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7312x4104+0+387/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F03%2F13%2Fa487f2ae407db04482883ab2e9ef%2Fstatesatty-120324-16.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d90846a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7312x4104+0+387/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F03%2F13%2Fa487f2ae407db04482883ab2e9ef%2Fstatesatty-120324-16.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7c6a1f7/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7312x4104+0+387/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F03%2F13%2Fa487f2ae407db04482883ab2e9ef%2Fstatesatty-120324-16.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Eileen O’Neill Burke after being sworn in as the Cook County state’s attorney in December 2024.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Ashlee Rezin/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>That commitment alone apparently didn’t appease the coalition led by Loevy and Loevy, which includes elected officials, community organizations, journalists and other attorneys.</p><p>Loevy and Loevy was involved in a case last year in which U.S. District Judge Sara Ellis took the extraordinary step of issuing a preliminary injunction to rein in the feds’ aggressive actions. The injunction was <a class="Link" href="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/b3/26/ced883424be7b53aa5b8d9586922/chc7thorderuse.pdf" target="_blank" ><u>formally vacated</u><b><u> </u></b><u>last week</u></a> by the 7th U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals.</p><p>Loevy and Loevy noted that sworn statements were submitted and hundreds of hours of video footage was compiled as part of federal court proceedings tied to Midway Blitz, indicating there’s already a pile of evidence waiting for a possible special prosecutor.</p><p>O’Neill Burke previously criticized Mayor Brandon Johnson's executive order directing the Chicago Police Department to go after federal agents, calling it "wholly inappropriate" because it instructed officers to make referrals to prosecutors "at the direction of the mayor's office." O'Neill Burke later released her own guidance last month. </p><p>Under the guidance, law enforcement agencies would lead criminal investigations by collecting and preserving evidence. Then, prosecutors would conduct an independent review of all evidence and make charging decisions, as they do in all felony cases. Her office said local law enforcement agencies haven't sought charges against any on-duty immigration officials.</p><p>O’Neill Burke noted on Monday that cases against the feds would be difficult to pursue, citing protections offered to on-duty agents and “challenges in acquiring evidence from federal law enforcement under this current administration.”</p><p>"My office’s goal is not to merely charge, but to successfully prosecute and convict criminal ICE agents,” O’Neill Burke said. “We are obligated to follow the law, evaluate evidence that is presented to us, and make a charging decision based on the facts.”</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/public-safety/2026/02/06/how-can-chicagos-top-prosecutor-hold-ice-accountable" target="_blank" >Veteran attorneys have been skeptical</a> about a judge’s ability to install a special prosecutor to investigate federal agents. Traditionally, special prosecutors are put in place when there is a conflict of interest or allegation of misconduct within the state’s attorney’s office.</p><p>The earlier petition for a special prosecutor, filed by attorney Sheryl Weikal, remains pending.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/02/19/chicago-ice-crime-border-patrol-trump-midway-blitz-alex-pretti-renee-good-eileen-oneill-burke" target="_blank" >Cook County’s top prosecutor outlines plan to charge ICE agents with crimes</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/public-safety/2026/02/06/how-can-chicagos-top-prosecutor-hold-ice-accountable" target="_blank" >How can Chicago’s top prosecutor hold ICE accountable?</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/02/06/eileen-oneill-burke-brandon-johnson-immigration-ice-law" target="_blank" >County’s top prosecutor calls Mayor Brandon Johnson’s ICE executive order ‘wholly inappropriate’</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Sophie Sherry

entries.title: Deerfield's Champaign dreams come true in supersectional win against St. Francis
entries.summary: <p>An awful lot has to go right for a basketball team to advance to the IHSA state finals. Since Deerfield opened in 1959, those circumstances never had come together.</p><p>The Warriors knew a run to Champaign in Class 3A this season was possible in November. On <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/high-school-basketball/scores/2026/03/09/ihsa-state-basketball-tournament-scores-illinois" target="_blank" >Monday</a> at NOW Arena in Hoffman Estates, senior star Jake Pollack and his teammates turned the dream into reality by defeating St. Francis 51-36 in the supersectional.</p><p>‘‘The first time in history,’’ Pollack said. ‘‘To be able to do this for our town, with all the fans here, is so special.’’</p><p>Deerfield’s quest for Champaign took on a different form this month. It went from one team’s dream to one town’s dream. During the past few weeks, Pollack and his teammates watched as their school and community started to embrace high school basketball.</p><p>‘‘The whole community in these last two weeks has been like nothing I’ve ever seen before,’’ Pollack said. ‘‘Everyone has been so active and so together.’’</p><p>Warriors coach Dan McKendrick said several Deerfield restaurants signed up for NFHS, the streaming service that carries the high school playoffs.</p><p>‘‘People want to go and get dinner and watch the game,’’ McKendrick said.</p><p>Deerfield will face undefeated Kaneland in the Class 3A semifinals Friday at the State Farm Center.</p><p>The Warriors (24-11) jumped out to an 18-5 lead, then nearly gave it all away by scoring only two points in the second quarter.</p><p>Senior guard Evan Nagler finished with 20 points, three rebounds and two blocks. His aggressive third quarter helped Deerfield take a 35-23 lead into the fourth. The Spartans (17-15) never challenged again.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-ed0000" name="image-ed0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0a4a21d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5494x3083+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F35%2F73%2Fdbf7be234190a64003699ffbda24%2Fob-cst-031026-009.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Deerfield’s Jake Pollack (11) smiles while hoisting the trophy after winning the 3A supersectional against St. Francis at NOW Arena.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Kirsten Stickney/For the Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Pollack, a 6-9 Dartmouth recruit, contributed 11 points, seven rebounds, two blocks and three steals. He scored seven points in the first quarter before facing intense defense from St. Francis’ Ben Whorlow, a 6-8 junior.</p><p>‘‘It’s really been a point to me that I need to set the tone for our team by going to the rim and being super-aggressive from the jump,’’ Pollack said. ‘‘When we come out and punch a team in the mouth like that, it is really hard to battle back and play from behind the whole game.’’</p><p></p><p>Sophomore Charlie Yellen added six points and junior Tommy Donahue five for the Warriors.</p><p></p><p>‘‘[Pollack] is the big brother,’’ McKendrick said. ‘‘The team feeds off his poise. He gets fouled more than anyone I’ve coached. He keeps his head and keeps going and keeps trying. He has done a really good job of being unselfish and allowing other kids to step up in big moments.’’</p><p></p><p>Senior Carter Clark led the Spartans with 10 points and six rebounds. Dylan Liner added six points and five rebounds and Johnny Shannon six points.</p><p></p><p>St. Francis took its lumps this season, finishing 1-7 in the rugged Catholic League Blue. The Spartans started the season 6-10, then advanced to the supersectional.</p><p></p><p>‘‘The character they have will help them the rest of their lives,’’ St. Francis coach Erin Dwyer said. ‘‘Every guy made adjustments this season to make us a better team. As a high school coach, you can’t ask for more.’’</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">Deerfield beats St. Francis 51-36. The Warriors are heading to the IHSA State Finals for the first time in school history. <br><br>Evan Nagler led with 20 points and Jake Pollack finished with 11 points and 7 rebounds. <a href="https://t.co/srXeYQ3lrd">pic.twitter.com/srXeYQ3lrd</a></p>— Michael O'Brien (@michaelsobrien) <a href="https://twitter.com/michaelsobrien/status/2031162745794519066?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 10, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p></p>

entries.title: Chicago sets record high temp Monday, 'severe' storms and 40s expected for Tuesday
entries.summary: <p>Monday’s sunny skies and warm temperatures were record breaking, according to the National Weather Service.</p><p>The official recorded high temperature at O'Hare International Airport was 73 degrees Monday, according to the NWS. The last record high for March 9 was 69 degrees in 2014, said Mark Ratzer, a meteorologist with the weather service.</p><p>The high for March 9 is typically about 44 degrees, Ratzer said.</p><p>But the warm weather is short lived. The Chicago area will return to “typical March” weather for the rest of the week, he said.</p><p>Temperatures drop to the 40s on Tuesday with a "severe" storm likely rolling in during the late afternoon and into the evening. That storm will likely bring rain, hail and strong winds across the Chicago area. The southern part of the metro area could also see tornadoes, Ratzer said.</p><p>By the end of the week, temperatures should slightly rise to the high 40s and low 50s. </p><div class="Enhancement" entries.title: Blackhawks pay tribute to Troy Murray
entries.summary: <p>The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks" >Blackhawks</a> paid tribute to <b>Troy Murray</b> on Monday.</p><p>Murray, a longtime Hawks player and broadcaster, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/03/07/troy-murray-dies-blackhawks-broadcaster-selke-trophy-winner" >died Saturday at age 63</a> after fighting cancer since 2021. Prior to the game against the Mammoth, the team’s first at home since Murray’s death, a video was played on the United Center videoboard and a moment of silence was held in his honor.</p><p>The 1986 Selke Trophy winner for being the NHL’s best defensive forward, Murray played 688 games with the Hawks. Arguably his most memorable moment as a player was the 1988-89 season finale against the Maple Leafs at Chicago Stadium.</p><p>The Hawks needed a point to make the playoffs and hold off Toronto for the final spot out of the old Norris Division. In overtime — during the era when teams did not earn a point for an OT loss — Murray stripped the puck from Leafs defenseman <b>Todd Gill </b>and beat goalie <b>Allan Bester </b>to <a class="Link" href="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Y2aIi7amOMY" target="_blank" >give the Hawks a 4-3 win</a> and propel them to the playoffs. The Hawks subsequently went on a surprise run to the Campbell Conference final but lost to the eventual Stanley Cup champion Flames.</p><p>In the radio booth for the Hawks’ three titles during the 2010s, Murray earned one as a player in 1996 with the Avalanche, appearing in eight playoff games to cap his NHL career with a championship. <b>Joel Quenneville</b>, who remembered Murray on Sunday, was an assistant coach on the Colorado team that swept the Panthers to win the Cup.</p><p>Quenneville told reporters in Anaheim that he and Murray drove back from airports during that run, and that he had the “pleasure” of being around him during the Hawks’ three successful Cup pushes.</p><p>“He’s one of those guys that, in the Blackhawk lore of good guys, he’s right up there,” said Quenneville, now coaching the Ducks.</p><p></p><h3><b>No Moore, Knight</b></h3><p>Forward <b>Oliver Moore </b>did not play Monday after crashing into the boards during Sunday’s overtime loss to the Stars. Moore was tangled up with Dallas center <b>Colin Blackwell</b>, slid into the boards<b> </b>and skated gingerly off the ice following the first-period goal by <b>Sam Rinzel.</b></p><p>Moore missed the last 51:19 of the game. Coach <b>Jeff Blashill </b>did not have much of an update on Moore, who went in for tests Monday.</p><p>Goalie <b>Spencer Knight </b>(illness) was also out. Knight has not played since a March 3 overtime loss to the Jets.</p><p><b>Drew Commesso </b>started in place of Knight.</p><p></p><h3><b>Rinzel getting run</b></h3><p>Entering Monday, Rinzel was averaging 21:59 of ice time and 24 shifts over the Hawks’ last four games.</p><p>Blashill said Rinzel is at his best when he’s making strong breakout passes that get the Hawks up the ice, where the young defenseman can join the play. Rinzel has done that of late, while managing the inherent risks of an offensive-minded defenseman.</p><p>“We talk all the time about making a play or living another day, and he’s managed that piece of the game well,” Blashill said. “What’s going to separate him in this league is the ability to skate and move the puck and he’s done a real good job of utilizing those powers that he has.”</p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" data-show-loadmore="true" ><a class="AnchorLink" entries.author: Brian Sandalow

entries.title: Cubs spring training camp looks different this year — literally
entries.summary: <p>MESA, Ariz. — Loop around the back of the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs" >Cubs</a>’ main practice field, onto the spring training complex’ patio, and the view across the agility field will be far different from years past.</p><p>Where there once was just more grass leading out to the bullpens and half-field, there now stands an 18,000-square-foot pitching and hitting lab. It extends nearly the length of the agility field, emblazoned with two Cubs logos and the team name.</p><p>“Camp’s never looked better,” chairman Tom Ricketts said earlier this spring.</p><p>His opinion, of course, doesn’t matter nearly as much as the opinions of the players and coaches using the facilities — and their reviews have been mostly glowing after some early growing pains with the labs’ layout.</p><p>The project, which also included renovations to the main building, was funded by the city of Mesa as part of a long-standing facilities agreement with the Cubs that allows the complex and stadium to also host a range of events outside of spring training.</p><p>The 6,400-square foot addition to the main training center includes a female staff locker room, an umpire locker room and multi-use office and meeting space.</p><p>The new lab houses two Trajekt pitching machines, plated mounds to measure the forces pitchers generate in their movements, plus plenty of other gadgets that measure biomechanical and pitch data. Flexible setups for the batting cages and mounds allow players and coaches to arrange these spaces based on their needs.</p><p>“The new building is incredible,” said second baseman Nico Hoerner, who has been in the Cubs organization since 2018. “It’s a really huge difference. Just thinking about how far tech and the ways that training in baseball have come, even since I’ve been with the Cubs, it’s really impressive. And it creates every opportunity for us to get ready for the season. It’s a great space.”</p><p>The Cubs were early adopters of the<a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs/2026/03/06/how-cubs-first-baseman-michael-busch-earned-everyday-role-at-bats-against-left-handed-pitchers" > Trajekt pitching machines</a>, which can be set to mimic specific pitchers’ arsenals. And Cubs hitting coach Dustin Kelly said the software and video quality has continued to improve through the years.</p><p>“We’ve been using them a lot more this spring than we had in the past,” Kelly said. “And I think that’s just kind of the evolution of the game and where we’re going, and how comfortable our guys are getting with tech. Just having those machines accessible, guys are using them more.”</p><p>Along with pitch data, the Cubs have been leaning more into force-plate data as an important tool for honing efficient delivery, which can improve both a pitcher’s performance and his health.</p><p>“You can see how much force you’re putting into the mound. You can see how much force you’re accepting with your land leg,” said veteran right-hander Jameson Taillon. “They can make skeletons of my body from my lab a week ago, compared to last year, compared to the year before, and they can overlay it all and show you, is your arm longer? Are you getting deeper into your back leg? Those kinds of things. It’s pretty cool.”</p><p>Left-hander Matthew Boyd sees the ability to precisely track movement as a draw for players at all stages of their careers.</p><p>“For a lot of these younger guys,” he said, “it’s like, ‘Hey, look at these time stamps of where you are. This is where you were really good. Let’s try to go back and replicate this.’ Or, ‘Look at how far you’ve grown’ — little things like that.</p><p>“[If] you think of someone who wants to come here and revitalize their career, it could be extremely beneficial.”</p><p>The new lab is also temperature-controlled — an especially big deal for players who’ll be rehabbing in Arizona in the summer, when temperatures can surpass 110 degrees for days on end.</p><p>“It seems super state-of-the-art,” said Taillon, who can compare to other facilities he used in his days with the Pirates and Yankees. “I haven’t played a ton of places, but it seems like we have to be [among] the top couple in the game.”</p><bsp-listcarousel class="PageListE" data-module data-column-count=3 data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" entries.title: On free agency's first day, Bears rework defense with focus on speed, versatility
entries.summary: <p>With holes to fill and modest money to spend, the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bears</a> spent the first wave of free agency Monday rebuilding their defense the way coordinator Dennis Allen wants it. With a focus on speed and versatility, the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/03/08/bears-transaction-tracker-2026-offseason-free-agency-coming-going-ryan-poles-ben-johnson" target="_blank" >Bears</a> added two starters and a rotation player — but could only watch as the price for premier pass-rushers skyrocketed.</p><p>Until the Bears can land an edge rusher to pair with Montez Sweat — and they look unlikely to do so in the first tier of free agency, barring an overpay to Trey Hendrickson — it will be impossible to know just how good Allen’s defense can be. The other two levels on the field began to look a lot more like his once-vaunted Saints defenses after Monday, though.</p><p>Bears general manager Ryan Poles and coach Ben Johnson agreed to sign former Seahawks <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/03/09/bears-sign-seahawks-coby-bryant-safety-nfl" >safety Coby Bryant, </a>a reigning Super Bowl champion, to a three-year deal worth $40 million. Like C.J. Gardner-Johnson, who has made stops on two different Allen teams, Bryant is a converted slot cornerback who provides the versatility Allen craves. He likes to mix and match cornerbacks and safeties on passing downs — and would have done it a lot more last season, were the Bears’ defensive backs healthier.</p><p>About an hour later, the Bears agreed to sign former Browns linebacker — and former Steelers’ first-round pick — <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/03/09/bears-to-sign-playmaking-veteran-devin-bush-jr-as-they-rebuild-linebacker-corps-source" >Devin Bush Jr.,</a> to a three-year, $30 million deal. The former No. 10 ovearll pick provides the speed Allen wants at the position — he ran a 4.43-second 40-yard dash at the NFL Scouting Combine.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/03/08/bears-transaction-tracker-2026-offseason-free-agency-coming-going-ryan-poles-ben-johnson" >Bears transaction tracker: Who’s coming, going this offseason</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>That’s a full second faster than Tremaine Edmunds, who used his size — at 6-foot-4, he’s five inches taller than Bush — to create mismatches in three seasons with the Bears. Poles decided to cut Edmunds on Thursday night and the linebacker <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/03/09/former-bears-linebacker-tremaine-edmunds-gets-3-year-36-million-deal-from-giants-report" >landed on his feet quickly,</a> agreeing to a three-year, $36 million deal to join the Giants on Monday.</p><p>"I just want to continue to improve our defensive speed,” Poles said at the NFL Scouting Combine, pointing out games down the stretch when the Bears weren’t fast enough.</p><p>D’Marco Jackson, who won NFC Defensive Player of the Week while substituting for Edmunds in Week 15, re-signed with the Bears on a two-year, $7.5 million contract. A waiver claim from the Saints at the end of training camp last year, Jackson has now played for Allen in both New Orleans and Chicago.</p><p>The Bears entered Monday with only two defensive tackles signed for 2026: Grady Jarrett and Gervon Dexter. They added to that number in the first hour of free agency, albeit with a modest move — they signed the Colts’ <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/03/09/bears-first-move-in-free-agency-is-deal-with-dt-neville-gallimore-source" >Neville Gallimore</a> to a two-year, $12 million contract. A Canadian whom the Cowboys drafted in the third round six years ago, Gallimore profiles as a rotational player. He’s started just 26 times in six years.</p><p>Gallimore had a career-high 3½ sacks last year. The Bears remain on patrol for pass-rush help, though, both from the end and tackle positions. They lost one of the former Monday when Dominique Robinson agreed to sign a one-year deal with the Texans.</p><p>But finding an edge rusher requires cap space the Bears might not be able to commit. The Panthers agreed to sign Eagles edge rusher Jaelan Phillips to a whopping four-year, $120  million deal, the largest of the day. Chargers edge rusher Odafe Oweh agreed to $100  million over four years, the third-largest. Two other edge rushers — Boye Mafe and Kwity Paye — agreed to larger contracts than the one the Bears gave Bryant, their largest splurge Monday.</p><p>Contracts can be signed officially starting at 3 p.m. Wednesday.</p><p>The Bears could pivot toward next month’s draft, where they hold three of the top 60 picks. Starter-quality defensive ends and tackles are expected to be available.</p><p>It remains to be seen whether signing Bryant ices out Kevin Byard, a team captain in 2025 and one of the most respected players in the Bears’ locker room. The two players’ skills could blend well, particularly given Allen’s penchant for juggling defensive backs, but it’s rare that a team pays serious money to both of its starting safeties. Byard led the NFL with seven interceptions last season, prompting talk of a renewed deal from both sides, but they had months to strike a deal and didn’t.</p><p>Bryant turns 27 next month. Bush turns 28 in July, and Gallimore turned 29 in January. The Bears hope all three can help buoy a run defense that was one of the NFL’s worst last year; only five teams gave up more rushing yards during the regular season. Pro Football Focus ranked Bush as the 10th-best run-defending linebacker in the NFL last season, while Bryant ranked 41st out of 122 safeties.</p><p>Time will tell whether they fit in on the field. At the least, Bryant’s new teammates won’t have a hard time remembering his name. He’s named after former Lakers star Kobe Bryant and wore No. 8 in his honor with the Seahawks. That will play well with the Bears’ offensive skill position players, who celebrate first downs by putting their thumbs and forefingers together the way Kobe Bryant did when he wanted to isolate a defender.</p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" data-show-loadmore="true" ><a class="AnchorLink" entries.title: Boy, 14, shot during argument in North Lawndale
entries.summary: <p>A teenage boy was listed in good condition after he was shot during an argument Monday in North Lawndale, police said.</p><p>The boy, 14, was arguing with someone who pulled a gun and fired shots then ran away, according to Chicago police. The shooting happened about 4:40 p.m. in the 3100 block of South Douglas Blvd.</p><p>The boy suffered a gunshot wound to the right wrist. He was taken to Humboldt Park Health in good condition.</p><p>Detectives are investigating and no one is in custody. No other information was available Monday evening. </p>

Enjoy your surroundings and, likewise, enjoy schmoozing with neighbors, siblings and relatives. Discussions about money, cash flow and earnings are likely. This is a good day to discuss your future goals with someone.</p><h2>Pisces (Feb. 19-March 20)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> People notice you today. You are sensitive, thoughtful and innovative. You believe in serving the common good. This is a year of exciting change, bringing you increased personal freedom. Let go of things that might be impeding your growth. Seek out new opportunities. Be ready to act fast. Trust your intuition. Prepare to travel.</p>
entries.author: Georgia Nicols

entries.title: Blackhawks forward Oliver Moore to miss "significant time"
entries.summary: <p>The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks" >Blackhawks</a> did not get good news on Oliver Moore.</p><p>Following Monday’s 3-2 overtime win over the Mammoth, Hawks coach Jeff Blashill said Moore will miss significant time and that the team will know more about the young forward’s prognosis in a few weeks. A first-round pick in the 2023 draft, Moore <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/03/08/blackhawks-stars-oliver-moore-injury-connor-bedard-nick-lardis" >crashed into the boards</a> during Sunday’s overtime loss to the Stars. He was tangled up with Dallas center Colin Blackwell, slid into the boards and skated gingerly off the ice following the first-period goal by Sam Rinzel.</p><p>“We’ll reevaluate as we go through the next month,” Blashill said.</p><p>Blashill’s revelation late Monday means it’s possible Moore has played his last game of the 2025-26 season. The Hawks’ season ends April 15, which would give Moore just over a month to return and continue his development at the NHL level.</p><p>In 51 games, Moore has five goals and 14 assists.</p>
entries.author: Brian Sandalow

entries.title: Chicago police shoot and kill person in Humboldt Park
entries.summary: <p>Chicago police shot and killed a person in Humboldt Park Monday while trying to pull over a vehicle wanted in a separate shooting investigation, police said.</p><p>Around 5:40 p.m., officers tried to stop a vehicle that was suspected of being involved in a reported shooting that occurred earlier Monday, according to Chicago police. The driver tried to flee and officers followed the vehicle in the 800 block of North Homan Avenue. As the person tried to flee, the car struck a pedestrian, an unmarked squad car and a CTA bus multiple times, police said.</p><p>As Chicago police officers tried to stop the vehicle to arrest the person, one of the officers fired shots, striking the person, police said. </p><p>Officers provided first aid to the person who was pronounced dead at Mount Sinai Hospital with multiple gunshot wounds to the upper body, police said. The person's gender, age and identity were not known as of late Monday.</p><p>Two officers were treated at a nearby hospital for minor injuries. The pedestrian hit by the car was taken to Mount Sinai Hospital, where she was listed in good condition.</p><p>Police say they recovered a gun from the car.</p><p>The shooting is under investigation by the Civilian Office of Police Accountability, which didn't respond to requests for comment Monday.</p>
entries.author: Mary Norkol

entries.title: Man, 75, charged with fatally shooting 2 women in Washington Heights
entries.summary: <p>A 75-year-old man is expected to appear in court Tuesday, accused of fatally shooting two women in Washington Heights on the South Side. </p><p>William Wallace Jr., of Chicago, was arrested about 20 minutes after the shooting Saturday night in the 10400 block of South Peoria Street and charged with two counts of first degree murder, Chicago police said. </p><p>Officers said they responded to a 911 call from Wallace about 11 p.m. Saturday, and when they knocked on the door of the home in the <a class="Link" href="https://www.suntimeswire.com/crime/2026/03/08/two-women-fatally-shot-washington-heights" target="_blank" >10400 block of South Peoria Street </a>he allegedly “spontaneously” told police that he shot the women and the gun was “upstairs,” according to a Chicago police report.</p><p>The officers found 68-year-old Phyllis Goolsby unresponsive with gunshot wounds to her chest on the second floor, according to the report. Goolsby and the alleged shooter were in a romantic relationship with no prior domestic incidents, the police report stated.</p><p>Officers said they found another victim, Tuanna J. Thomas, 71, unresponsive in the first-floor bedroom with gunshot wounds to her back and torso, the report said.</p><p>Wallace was scheduled to appear for a detention hearing Tuesday. </p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Blackhawks prospect John Mustard has fleshed out his game with better shooting, decision-making
entries.summary: <p>Entering last summer, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks" target="_blank" >Blackhawks</a> forward prospect John Mustard's finishing skills were holding him back from being an elite college player.</p><p>Instead of simply firing a million pucks at an empty net every day, Mustard raised the difficulty level, training with veteran NHL goalie Alex Lyon — who has become a big part of the Sabres' surprising success this season, posting a .915 save percentage in 30 games.</p><p>Over time, Mustard learned how to pick the right spots on Lyon, and that has transformed him into a terror for college goalies during his sophomore season at Providence.</p><p>"I’m starting to read goalies a little bit and take more high-percentage shots," Mustard said recently. "[Lyon] really helped me out. It’s pretty hard to score on him. [I'm] trying to work on getting shots through on him."</p><p>Mustard's shot has become one of his stronger attributes. He has 15 goals in 34 games this season, up from seven goals in 37 games <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2025/07/02/blackhawks-nhl-prospects-john-mustard-providence-julius-sumpf" target="_blank" >last season</a>. His shooting percentage has jumped from 6.5% to 11.6%.</p><p>Since getting drafted in the third round in 2024, he has flown somewhat under the radar within the Hawks' massive prospect pool, but that needs to change soon. At 19 years old, he looks like a probable future NHL player. Hawks assistant general manager Mark Eaton, who oversees the prospects, has been generous with praise.</p><p>Mustard's speed was his calling card when drafted, and it remains a crucial ingredient in his game. The difference is he has fleshed out everything else, and his brain has figured out how to keep up with his legs. Eaton mentioned his decision-making as another attribute that has improved.</p><p>"When you’re moving at a high speed, it’s hard to make plays," Mustard said. "Lanes close pretty quickly. That’s something I’ve had to learn over time.</p><p>"It’s just about keeping my eyes up. It’s not just thinking 'shot' all the time. It’s working off guys and reading off them."</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">John Mustard’s most recent goal on Feb. 28 was quite a highlight: <a href="https://t.co/4xSVGOglpZ">pic.twitter.com/4xSVGOglpZ</a></p>— Ben Pope’s Video Clips (@BenPopeCSTclips) <a href="https://twitter.com/BenPopeCSTclips/status/2031236460364575083?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 10, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>A back injury slowed him down for a few weeks in January, but he played through it and considered it a useful opportunity to "learn what my B-game was." </p><p>He's fully healthy now and has scored in five of his last eight games. He has skated a decent amount lately on the same line (on opposite wings) as fellow Hawks prospect Julius Sumpf, a 2025 fourth-round pick and overage freshman at Providence.</p><p>Pucks haven't gone in much for Sumpf this season — he has four goals and 17 assists — so he actually has looked to Mustard for advice on that front, which exemplifies just how much Mustard's shot has improved.</p><p>"Whenever you give [John] the puck in the offensive zone, he’s a threat," Sumpf said. "Everyone has to respect his shot...[and] his speed is unbelievable sometimes. He can beat everyone down the side. Every defenseman has to be aware of him."</p><p>Mustard, in turn, raved about Sumpf's intelligence and reliability, noting how he "almost never makes the wrong play." The Hawks have also liked how much Sumpf, who turned 21 in January, has improved this season.</p><p>"Usually you don’t mean it literally when you say it, but [Sumpf] has literally gotten better every single game," Eaton said. </p><p>"He’s not super-physical or a speed demon or super-skilled, but he’s a coach’s dream. He’s such a responsible player: always in the right position, always on the right side of the puck. And his strength on his skates allows him to be hard to play against."</p><p>Providence enjoyed an excellent regular season as a team and has a good chance to make a deep NCAA Tournament run, which would offer Mustard and Sumpf more high-intensity experience.</p><p>It sounds like Mustard will likely return for his junior year in 2026-27, although he and the Hawks will discuss plans further after Providence's season ends.</p>
entries.author: Ben Pope

entries.title: Man shot to death in Englewood apartment
entries.summary: <p>A man was found shot to death late Monday in the hallway of an Englewood apartment on the South Side. </p><p>Around 11:45 p.m., officers responded to a call for service at an apartment building in the 800 block of West 66th Street where 24-year-old Terrell M. Britten was found in a hallway unresponsive with gunshot wounds to his face and abdomen, Chicago police and the Cook County medical examiner's office said. </p><p>Britten, of the 6700 block of South Sangamon Street, was pronounced dead at 11:50 p.m. at the scene, the medical examiner's office said.</p><p>A weapon was recovered at the scene, authorities said. </p><p>Area 1 detectives were investigating. </p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Dear Abby: Observer thinks woman is lying about medical condition
entries.summary: <p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> I have an acquaintance, "Tina," who I believe is fraudulently using her GoFundMe fundraising webpage. A close friend of hers set it up a few years ago to assist with medical bills related to a rare form of cancer that will never fully go away. When the page began, Tina was soliciting donations on Facebook and sending text messages to remind everyone to donate. Later on, when she and her husband went out of town for surgery, they posted about it as if it were a vacation.</p><p>Recently, she has had some legal bills for an unrelated matter, and a year after her last medical donation, she received a hefty donation that matched the amount of her legal bills. I suspect the person who donated the money was duped into believing Tina had another large medical bill, but she used that donation for her legal expenses.</p><p>On top of this, Tina has opened a new fundraising page with a sob story about her cancer returning and being hospitalized about a week before that request. Abby, I know full well Tina was healthy as an ox at the time, was not in the hospital and probably set this up to create a smokescreen to protect her malicious intentions. Now I keep wondering if I should report it, or if I'm better off minding my own business. — <i>NOT ADDING UP IN FLORIDA</i></p><p><b>DEAR NOT ADDING UP:</b> If you sincerely think Tina has been abusing her GoFundMe page, report it. The process is not difficult. There is a notification button at the bottom of the page that can be pushed to alert its "Fraud Squad" that something shady might be going on.</p><p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> I've been married to the same man for nearly 50 years. We raised a sweet family, had successful careers and never strayed from each other. For the last 20-plus years, I've had little or no desire for sex. I understand it medically as well as emotionally, but Hubby doesn't get it at all. He thinks we should be like we were back in the '70s and '80s.</p><p>I'm happy for senior couples who engage in healthy sexual relationships. I am just no longer into it and, honestly, also not interested in taking medication to "enhance" me. What's wrong with a man taking something to "unenhance" himself? I know the biblical duty God gave for procreation, hence 100-year-old men fathering children with young women. The balance has never been BALANCED.</p><p>Yes, I had a hysterectomy 20 years ago. After that, I lost all interest, yet Hubby still had needs. Some are met; many are not. It is now a BIG issue. I'd like to sail peacefully through the next 10 years, if I have that long. I'd like him to find happiness, too. Is it crazy to divorce over such an age-old need for a man? — <i>HAPPY THIS WAY IN SOUTH CAROLINA</i></p><p><b>DEAR HAPPY:</b> "Castrating" your husband is not the answer. If you two can agree on an arrangement that will benefit both of you, it would be emotionally and financially far less disruptive than a divorce.</p><p><i>Dear Abby is written by Abigail Van Buren, also known as Jeanne Phillips, and was founded by her mother, Pauline Phillips. Contact Dear Abby at www.DearAbby.com or P.O. Box 69440, Los Angeles, CA 90069.</i></p><p>For an excellent guide to becoming a better conversationalist and a more sociable person, order "How to Be Popular." Send your name and mailing address, plus check or money order for $8 (U.S. funds) to: Dear Abby, Popularity Booklet, P.O. Box 446, Kings Mills, OH 45034-0446. (Shipping and handling are included in the price.)</p>
entries.author: Abigail Van Buren

entries.title: Discussing white 'advantage' doesn't diminish white working-class' struggles
entries.summary: <p>Last week I <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/columnists/2026/03/03/jesse-jackson-racism-black-white-civil-rights-rainbow-coalition-ben-jealous" >wrote</a> about something my father, who is white, has said for years: when many white Americans hear the phrase "white privilege," they respond by listing all the ways their lives have been hard. </p><p>But if you talk about "white advantage," the conversation changes. Advantage is measurable. It doesn’t erase suffering. It simply acknowledges that race still tilts the system.</p><p>After that column ran, readers reached out asking me to elaborate.</p><p>To understand why, we have to go back to the beginning of the American story.</p><p>Early in colonial America, poor Europeans and enslaved Africans sometimes recognized that they shared a common predicament. They ran away together. They rebelled together. For a brief moment in our history, working people across color lines sometimes saw each other as allies rather than enemies.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-600000" name="module-600000"></a> <div class="RichTextSidebarModule-title">Columnist </div> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody">Columnist</div> </div><p>That possibility deeply unsettled the people who held power. So over time, colonial elites built a system designed to prevent that unity. Europeans were given small advantages — permission to carry weapons, positions in militias, small measures of authority over enslaved Africans. At the same time, racial contempt was deliberately cultivated between the groups.</p><p>Racism itself became a tool of political control. The wedge worked. In many ways, it still does.</p><p>White communities across America are hurting. Factories have closed. Life expectancy for many working-class Americans has fallen. Addiction and suicide have devastated entire towns.</p><p>Acknowledging white advantage doesn’t deny any of that.</p><p>It simply says this: if two people walk into the same job interview with the same resume, race still affects the outcome. If two families try to build wealth across generations, race still shapes the odds. Social scientists have measured this for years. In a well-known study, Princeton sociologist Devah Pager sent out identical resumes to employers.</p><p>Applicants with traditionally white-sounding names received far more callbacks than those with Black-sounding names, even when their qualifications were the same.</p><p>Suffering and advantage can exist at the same time.</p><p>My father understood something many activists forget: language can open conversations, or it can shut them down.</p><p>For decades he worked with thousands of men — mostly white — helping them confront trauma and take responsibility for their actions. He saw how quickly people shut down when they feel their suffering is being dismissed.</p><p>That’s the predicament. The reality of racial advantage is undeniable. But the language we use to describe it sometimes pushes away the very people who need to be part of the solution.</p><p>The Rev. Jesse Jackson understood this better than anyone. His Rainbow Coalition was built on a simple insight: racism has long been the oldest political wedge in America. Divide working people by race and those in power stay secure. But if working people across racial lines ever truly unite, the coalition would be powerful enough to transform the country.</p><p>The opposite of racism isn’t just tolerance. It’s solidarity. Our history shows both possibilities. At times we have been divided by race so completely that we could barely see our shared interests. At other moments — Reconstruction, the labor movement, the Civil Rights era — we have glimpsed what multiracial democracy can look like.</p><p>That history should give us hope. Because if racism was built to divide us, it can also be dismantled.</p><p>Jackson spent a lifetime trying to show us what comes next. When working people finally refuse the wedge — when we stand together from union halls to houses of worship, from big cities to small towns — the coalition that emerges will be stronger than the politics that have kept us apart for generations.</p><p><i>Ben Jealous is a professor of practice at the University of Pennsylvania and former president and CEO of the NAACP.</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-290000" name="module-290000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody">Send letters to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. More about <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/submitting-opinion-and-letters" >how to submit here</a>.</div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: Ben Jealous

entries.title: Another Trump administration deportation blitz hasn’t materialized in Chicago
entries.summary: <p>When U.S. Border Patrol Cmdr. Gregory Bovino<b> </b>left town last fall, Chicago’s reprieve came with a warning: The tear gas, chaos and fear associated with Operation Midway Blitz could return to the city’s streets<b> </b>fourfold<b> </b>in March.</p><p>Now March is here. And President Donald Trump has <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/politics/2026/03/05/trump-fires-noem-dhs-ice-border-patrol-markwayne-mullin" target="_blank" >fired Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem</a>, instead.</p><p>Bovino’s out too. The White House put him on the sidelines in January after the fatal shootings of two U.S. citizens by federal officers<b> </b>in Minneapolis. Even Tricia<b> </b>McLaughlin<b>, </b>the assistant secretary who once fiercely defended Homeland Security, has moved on.</p><p>All told, there’s little on the horizon to suggest Operation Midway Blitz 2.0 is about to materialize.</p><p>Much has changed since a source told the Chicago Sun-Times<b> </b>in November<b> </b>that <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/public-safety/2025/11/11/u-s-border-patrol-boss-greg-bovino-fellow-agents-expected-to-soon-leave-chicago" target="_blank" >1,000 agents could return</a><b> </b>this month.<b> </b>The enforcement surge in Minnesota that followed the earlier Chicago campaign<b> </b>turned out to be a breaking point, leaving the Trump administration struggling to justify the shooting deaths of Renee Good and Alex Pretti.</p><p>Calls to reform immigration enforcement<b> </b>have led to a partial government shutdown. Senate Democrats last week blocked<b> </b>Homeland Security funding<b> </b>for a third time,<b> </b>refusing to approve a spending bill without<b> </b>stricter use-of-force policies and a ban on officers wearing masks.</p><p>Steve Art, an attorney<b> </b>who challenged the feds’ tactics<b> </b>in court last fall, said the Minneapolis campaign gave the American people<b> </b>"a look at what we saw in Chicago throughout Operation Midway Blitz."</p><p>And, he said,<b> </b>"the American people didn’t like it."</p><p>The Chicago operation featured roving bands of masked agents, thousands of arrests, the seemingly indiscriminate use of tear gas and pepper balls, and two shootings, one of which claimed the life of <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2025/09/12/ice-officer-fatally-shoots-man-during-traffic-stop-in-chicago-suburb-authorities-say" target="_blank" >Silverio Villegas González</a>. </p><p>"Bovino being gone and Noem being gone within six months of the start of Operation Midway Blitz really shows you how politically untenable it is to engage in that kind of operation in the United States," Art said. "And that should give everybody a lot of hope."</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-ac0000" name="html-embed-module-ac0000"></a><div class="flourish-embed flourish-chart" data-src="visualisation/27969115"><script src="https://public.flourish.studio/resources/embed.js"></script><noscript><img src="https://public.flourish.studio/visualisation/27969115/thumbnail" width="100%" alt="visualization" /></noscript></div> </div> </div><p>A Homeland Security spokesperson said officials in the department "do not discuss future or potential operations."</p><p>"Every day, DHS enforces the laws of the nation across the country including in Chicago," the spokesperson added.</p><p>Immigration enforcement remains a key pillar of Trump’s second term. U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement has a mountain of funding and continues to hire and increase its capacity<b> </b>to remove immigrants from the country, according to a former Homeland Security official.</p><p>The feds are still fighting a court order that would govern the treatment of people held<b> </b>in an immigration processing facility<b> </b>in Broadview. Last fall, a judge heard<b> </b>that<b> </b>people were crammed<b> </b>100 at a time<b> </b>into a cell there, with nowhere to sleep<b> </b>but a dirty floor<b> </b>near an open toilet.</p><p>Art said it’s good to be prepared for another surge in enforcement. But he also warned of future issues "relating to the election and to polling places." </p><p>Meanwhile, in the nation’s capital, U.S. Rep. Delia Ramirez<b> </b>said Democrats<b> </b>"can’t keep sabotaging ourselves." She took Noem to task<b> </b>in a fiery meeting<b> </b>last month<b> </b>and told her, "your days may be numbered."</p><p>That was eight days before<b> </b>Trump showed Noem the door.</p><p>Now, with Homeland Security on its heels, Ramirez told the Sun-Times "this is our moment."</p><p>"Our strategy has to be, delay as far as you possibly can," the Illinois Democrat said. "Delay till November, if you can. Maybe January. And then let us get into the majority."</p><h3><b>Changing tactics</b></h3><p>The public outcry<b> </b>that stifled the feds’ immigration operation<b> </b>in Minneapolis<b> </b>led to<b> </b>new<b> </b>ICE directives<b> </b>that were relayed during<b> </b>recent<b> </b>agency-wide leadership meetings, the former Homeland Security official<b> </b>told the Sun-Times.</p><p>Leaders were told to prioritize<b> </b>"targeted enforcement,"<b> </b>instead of the "street sweeps" and large-scale campaigns like Midway Blitz, the official said. That would mean a return to ICE’s traditional, but still aggressive, enforcement tactics.</p><p>It would also mean setting aside the hallmark, but controversial, tactics of Midway Blitz: Roaming Chicago’s neighborhoods and stopping anyone whose legal status appeared to be in doubt. Those tactics came roaring back Dec. 16, when Bovino led Border Patrol agents on a brief trip to the Chicago area.</p><p>ICE still has vast financial resources and access to vital information from other government agencies that helps agents identify and target people.</p><p>“Does the Chicago field office just really try to do a lot of enforcement without supplemental folks from other areas and other federal agencies?” the former official said. “Do they look at different ways of arresting people, like worksites?”</p><p>“It doesn’t appear that they’re going to stop doing immigration enforcement because they’re ramping up all the other parts of the system.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-0f0001" name="image-0f0001"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d7e0353/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1680x943+0+89/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F69%2F23%2Ff352d7274ea48253f2e36516165c%2Fblitznight.jpeg data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Federal immigration enforcement agents detain a man during a skirmish outside the ICE facility in Broadview on Sept. 26, 2025. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>There’s no obvious sign of another Chicago surge within Homeland Security spending, though. Last summer,<b> </b>the agency stocked up on office supplies and ammunition to be used<b> </b>here, while also investing in relocation services for agents, according to federal data.</p><p>Similar purchases were made in Minnesota and North Carolina in the months leading up to increased immigration enforcement<b> </b>in those states.</p><p>The department has continued buying supplies and security equipment in Minnesota over the past month, as large swaths of agents remain in the area.</p><p>But the department<b> </b>hasn’t made any major purchases<b> </b>for use<b> </b>here<b> </b>since the withdrawal<b> </b>of Bovino and many of his agents<b> </b>in November.</p><p>The last contract<b> </b>the department signed<b> </b>related to the Broadview holding facility<b> </b>was a $349,000<b> </b>purchase<b> </b>for box lunch services<b> </b>on Dec. 8.</p><p>Those services are set to expire in early April.</p><h3><b>Plans for polling places?</b></h3><p>The spending impasse in Washington leaves immigration enforcement largely unaffected. That’s because the agencies at the crux of the argument are considered essential under a shutdown.</p><p>That includes ICE, which received<b> </b>$75 billion<b> </b>in Trump’s major tax and spending bill, known as the One Big Beautiful Bill Act. That also allows the agency to ride out the funding lapse while other departments under Homeland Security will be forced to furlough workers and cut operations.</p><p>Chicago Democrats<b> </b>like Ramirez<b> </b>hope the impasse — and Noem’s firing — will delay any plans to target Chicago in another sweeping immigration operation. Trump said he would nominate U.S. Sen. Markwayne<b> </b>Mullin, R-Oklahoma, as his next Homeland Security secretary.</p><p>But Ramirez said Mullin<b> </b>is unlikely to be confirmed by the Senate until some time in April.</p><p>“Frankly, it wouldn't work well with them if they end up continuing these big operations without a leader at the helm,” Ramirez said.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-770000" name="image-770000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a114561/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+369/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F58%2Ff5%2F8f8dbe7043c58d30c510eeddd0e5%2Fjohnsonice-061825-13.jpg" 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a114561/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+369/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F58%2Ff5%2F8f8dbe7043c58d30c510eeddd0e5%2Fjohnsonice-061825-13.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>U.S. Rep. Delia Ramirez speaks during a World Refugee Day ceremony at Daley Plaza on June 18, 2025.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Pat Nabong/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Lawmakers in the nation’s capital have also raised concerns about the Trump administration using ICE or Border Patrol agents to disrupt voting in November. U.S. Sen. Chris<b> </b>Coons<b> </b>asked Noem last week, before she was fired, whether she would rule out<b> </b>deploying agents<b> </b>to polling places.</p><p>“There are no plans to have ICE officers at our polling locations,” Noem told the Delaware<b> </b>Democrat.</p><p>Coons cited a law<b> </b>forbidding troops at the polls<b> </b>and continued to press Noem on whether she would “rule it out.”<b> </b>Noem retorted, “do you plan on illegal aliens voting in our elections, senator?”</p><p>Others share Coons’ concern. Art said plans are in the works<b> </b>to litigate<b> </b>“whether there’s ever a reason to have federal forces, like we saw during Operation Midway Blitz, anywhere near a polling place.”</p><p>“We think the obvious answer to that, under the law, is no,” Art said.<b> </b>“But it’s our intention to litigate that issue far in advance.”</p><p>Art helped win<b> </b>last fall’s<b> </b>order<b> </b>from U.S. District Judge<b> </b>Sara Ellis<b> </b><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2025/11/06/judge-sara-ellis-operation-midway-blitz-tear-gas-deportation-campaign" target="_blank" >restricting<b> </b>the feds’ use of force</a> during<b> </b>Midway Blitz — an order <a class="Link" href="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/b3/26/ced883424be7b53aa5b8d9586922/chc7thorderuse.pdf" target="_blank" >formally vacated<b> </b>last week</a> by the 7th U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals. Art's team also secured<b> </b>an under-oath deposition<b> </b>from Bovino, during which Ellis said<b> </b>he lied<b> </b>about his justification<b> </b>for the use of tear gas<b> </b>in Little Village.</p><h3><b>‘They say they’re coming back’</b></h3><p>The ICE holding facility in Broadview became a focal point of Midway Blitz. Not only did many of the feds’ detainees find themselves held there at some point, but it became the site of near-daily protests.</p><p>Designed as a short-term holding facility, U.S. District Judge Robert<b> </b>Gettleman<b> </b>remarked last November that it had <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2025/11/04/claims-of-unsanitary-black-hole-conditions-at-broadviews-ice-facility-to-be-heard-by-federal-judge" target="_blank" >“really become a prison.”</a><b> </b>He did so while presiding over a lawsuit that alleges the feds were “warehousing people at Broadview for days on end.”</p><p>Gettleman handed down a temporary restraining order<b> </b>that month<b> </b>aimed at improving conditions inside the facility.<b> </b>He required<b> </b>a clean mat<b> </b>and bedding<b> </b>for everyone<b> </b>held there overnight,<b> </b>and that cells<b> </b>be cleaned<b> </b>twice daily.</p><p>The order is still in effect. A two-day<b> </b>hearing<b> </b>over whether Gettleman should enter a more permanent order<b> </b>is set to begin April 21.</p><p>But in mid-December, a month after Bovino and his agents first left town, the Justice Department seemed to have an opportunity to bring the case to an end. DOJ lawyer Jana<b> </b>Brady<b> </b>told the judge that “Broadview is back to being just a processing center.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-920000" name="image-920000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4578f07/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F4b%2F24%2Fe440d6c84aa79efbe46ac02b05a2%2Fbroadview-092325-18.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e39d4b0/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F4b%2F24%2Fe440d6c84aa79efbe46ac02b05a2%2Fbroadview-092325-18.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7db9b10/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F4b%2F24%2Fe440d6c84aa79efbe46ac02b05a2%2Fbroadview-092325-18.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="BROADVIEW-092325-18.jpg" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7db9b10/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F4b%2F24%2Fe440d6c84aa79efbe46ac02b05a2%2Fbroadview-092325-18.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ff5aa62/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F4b%2F24%2Fe440d6c84aa79efbe46ac02b05a2%2Fbroadview-092325-18.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7db9b10/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F4b%2F24%2Fe440d6c84aa79efbe46ac02b05a2%2Fbroadview-092325-18.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>A pair of federal agents stand atop the immigrant processing center in suburban Broadview on Sept. 22, 2025. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>She said the case was<b> </b>“moot.”<b> </b>In other words, there was no reason to keep arguing about it. The controversy was over.</p><p>But Gettleman pressed her on the question on many Chicagoans’ minds. He said he would consider whether the case had become irrelevant<b> </b>if Brady would only “stipulate”<b> </b>that a second surge in immigration enforcement was not in the works.</p><p>“If the government learns that we’re not going to have a surge again, and is able to give assurances about that, then I would be open to some sort of resolution,” Gettleman told her.</p><p>“What you’re asking for is law-enforcement-sensitive information,” Brady replied. She said it could lead to the “thwarting”<b> </b>of a mission or “safety issues for all involved.”</p><p>So the case is still ongoing, three months later.</p><p>Gettleman told Brady he was simply taking her client at its word.</p><p>“They say they’re coming back here in force,” he told her.</p><p><i>Contributing: Tom Schuba</i></p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/politics/2026/03/05/trump-fires-noem-dhs-ice-border-patrol-markwayne-mullin" target="_blank" >Trump ousts Kristi Noem, Pritzker says ‘don’t let the door hit you on the way out’</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/03/dhs-kristi-noem-senate-judiciary-committee-testimony-marimar-martinez-dick-durbin" target="_blank" >DHS head Noem claims she doesn’t know about Marimar Martinez, Chicago woman shot 5 times by federal agent</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2025/11/04/claims-of-unsanitary-black-hole-conditions-at-broadviews-ice-facility-to-be-heard-by-federal-judge" target="_blank" >Federal judge likely to intervene at Broadview’s ICE facility: ‘It has really become a prison’</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Sophie Sherry

entries.title: Owner of Middle Eastern restaurant Old School in Palos Hills brings a personal touch to dining
entries.summary: <p>At Old School, a Middle Eastern restaurant specializing in lamb dishes in suburban Palos Hills, dinner often begins with an early-morning message in a WhatsApp group chat.</p><p>Each morning, owner and chef Mohd Mahd sends his roughly 70 regulars a list of what he is cooking that day. They reply with orders for pickup, reserving most of the restaurant’s roughly 60-100 daily meals before the doors even open. There is no fixed menu and the business does not operate like a typical dine-in or carryout restaurant. What is offered changes day to day and is dictated by customers who span the Middle Eastern diaspora. Dish requests can be put in for the following day by anyone. Walk-ins are welcome and are offered a menu set by the previous day’s requests. The most expensive dish tops out at $32 for a 3-pound, bone-in lamb.</p><p>The restaurant has gained a loyal following (with 730 reviews on Google, it has an almost-perfect 4.9 star rating) for its unconventional business.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-8c0000" name="image-8c0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" alt="Interior of Old School in Palos Hills on Friday, 6 March " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1e9e908/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4128x2317+0+218/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F5f%2F9e%2F2b865408447a9b641aa2975de6d4%2Framadanfood-260311-14.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/cc90ebf/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4128x2317+0+218/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F5f%2F9e%2F2b865408447a9b641aa2975de6d4%2Framadanfood-260311-14.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1e9e908/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4128x2317+0+218/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F5f%2F9e%2F2b865408447a9b641aa2975de6d4%2Framadanfood-260311-14.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Old School does not operate like a typical restaurant. There is no fixed menu and what is offered changes day to day and is dictated by customers. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Timothy Hiatt/For the Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“People can expect somebody that’s cooking this food with passion, with honesty, no cheating, the right amount, the right way, and the right flavor,” said Sammy Daaboul, of Orland Park, and a customer since 2017. “What he promises he fulfills, bottom line you don't like it, he will say, ‘I'm sorry. Here’s your money back.’ He’ll give you a rain check for another dish to give it one more try. But 99.9% once you enter his premises, you are hooked.”</p><p>However, diners considering a first visit may want to wait until after Ramadan, the restaurant’s busiest season.</p><p>During the ninth and holiest month of the Islamic calendar, when Muslims fast from sunrise to sunset, Old School operates less as a traditional eatery and more like what Mahd calls a “food club.” He limits his choose-your-own-adventure ordering format to regular customers only in order to meet the surge in demand for catering orders and the rush to have everything ready by <i>iftar</i>, the evening meal after breaking fast. Outside of Ramadan, Old School prepares an average of 125-150 meals a day for the restaurant and catering orders. That number jumps to 400 and can go as high as 600 during the holy month.</p><p>“Most of my customers expect their food to be ready around sunset,” Mahd said. “They let me know as early as possible what they want, because they know the later they call the least amount of options they would get. Usually around four o’clock, I’m already all reserved. There’s nothing to offer.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-aa0000" name="image-aa0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/da7b595/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4128x2317+0+218/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F50%2F18%2F9181f6d0493e91e3ac77d63df083%2Framadanfood-260311-12.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/821f002/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4128x2317+0+218/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F50%2F18%2F9181f6d0493e91e3ac77d63df083%2Framadanfood-260311-12.jpg
